name: Tadalafil improved brachial flow-mediated dilation in a small cardiovascular-risk ED trial
statement: In a small randomized trial of men with ED and increased cardiovascular risk, tadalafil 20 mg every other day improved brachial artery flow-mediated dilation and shifted nitrite/nitrate and endothelin-1 biomarkers versus baseline, with benefit sustained 2 weeks after stopping.
population_or_context: Men with ED and increased cardiovascular risk.
quantitative_note: FMD improved with tadalafil from 4.2+/-3.2% to 9.3+/-3.7% at 4 weeks (P<0.01 vs baseline), while placebo was unchanged from 4.1+/-2.8% to 4.0+/-3.4%; tadalafil FMD remained 9.1+/-3.9% at 6 weeks.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Abstract reports FMD, nitrite/nitrate, and endothelin-1 changes after tadalafil and placebo.

caveats: ED and increased cardiovascular-risk population, not healthy users.
Surrogate endothelial markers do not establish cardiovascular event reduction.
population_profile: Men with ED and increased cardiovascular risk; indirect for mostly healthy men age 30 to 50.
tadalafil_exposure: Tadalafil 20 mg every other day for 4 weeks.
indication_context: Experimental endothelial-function trial in ED/increased cardiovascular risk.
endpoint_measure: Brachial artery flow-mediated dilation, plasma nitrite/nitrate, and endothelin-1.
effect_estimate: FMD changed from 4.2+/-3.2% to 9.3+/-3.7% with tadalafil, versus no placebo change; sustained 9.1+/-3.9% at 6 weeks.
safety_findings: Abstract-level extract does not report adverse-event rates; use tadalafil safety baseline for interaction and tolerability boundaries.
directness_to_target_population: Low to moderate for tadalafil vascular surrogate effects; low for healthy 30-50 clinical cardiovascular benefit.
confounding_and_bias_limits: Small trial, surrogate endpoints, ED/CV-risk population, short follow-up, and no clinical cardiovascular endpoints.
class_transfer_limits: Direct tadalafil evidence; no class transfer needed, but population and surrogate endpoint transfer is limited.
